questions.question: Explain the concept of Use Case in software engineering and state its primary purpose.
keyPoints: Definition of Use Case: technique for capturing functional requirements from user perspective
Developed by Ivar Jacobson in 1980s
Adopted through UML
Purpose: bridges gap between user needs and technical implementation
Captures system behavior from external actor's perspective

questions.question: Differentiate between Primary Actor and Secondary Actor with an example.
keyPoints: Primary Actor: initiates use case, achieves goal through it (e.g., Customer, Bank Patron)
Secondary Actor: supports system to fulfill use case (e.g., Database Administrator, Payment Gateway)
Primary actors are beneficiaries; Secondary actors provide services
Example: In ATM withdrawal, Customer is primary, Bank system is secondary

questions.question: What is the 'include' relationship in use cases? When should it be used? Give an example.
keyPoints: Include relationship: one use case incorporates behavior of another
Used when common behavior needs to be reused across multiple use cases
Reduces duplication of similar steps
Example: 'Validate User' included in 'Withdraw Cash', 'Transfer Money', 'Check Balance'
Shown as dashed arrow with <<include>> stereotype

questions.question: What is the 'extend' relationship in use cases? When is it appropriate to use?
keyPoints: Extend relationship: adds behavior to base use case under certain conditions
Used for optional or exceptional behavior not part of basic flow
Used when certain conditions trigger additional functionality
Example: 'Print Receipt' extends 'Withdraw Cash' when user requests receipt
Shown as dashed arrow with <<extend>> stereotype

questions.question: List and explain the main components of a use case description.
keyPoints: Use Case Name: identifies the use case
Actors: who participates in the use case
Preconditions: conditions that must be true before use case starts
Basic Flow: ideal sequence of steps (happy path)
Alternative Flows: alternate or exceptional paths
Postconditions: conditions guaranteed after successful completion
Business Rules: constraints or rules governing the use case

questions.question: Explain the difference between Precondition and Postcondition in use case description.
keyPoints: Precondition: conditions that must be true BEFORE the use case begins
Defines system state or environment required to start
Example: User must be logged in before checking account balance
Postcondition: conditions guaranteed to be TRUE AFTER successful completion
Defines what has been achieved or changed
Example: Account balance is updated after withdrawal

questions.question: What is generalization in use cases? How is it represented in UML?
keyPoints: Generalization: inheritance relationship between use cases or actors
Represents 'is-a' relationship (specialization)
Child inherits behavior of parent and adds/modifies
In actors: 'Customer' is generalization of 'Registered Customer'
In use cases: 'Make Payment' generalization of 'Credit Card Payment'
Shown as solid arrow with triangular arrowhead pointing to parent

questions.question: Draw and explain a use case diagram for an Online Shopping System. Identify at least 5 use cases and 2 actors. Show include and extend relationships.
keyPoints: Diagram with system boundary rectangle
Actors: Customer, Administrator (or Payment System)
Use Cases: Browse Products, Add to Cart, Place Order, Make Payment, Track Order, Manage Products
Include: 'Process Payment' included in 'Place Order'
Extend: 'Apply Discount' extends 'Place Order' when promo code used
Associations: lines connecting actors to relevant use cases
Proper UML notation with stereotypes

questions.question: Write a detailed use case description for 'Withdraw Cash' from an ATM system. Include all components of a use case description.
keyPoints: Use Case Name: Withdraw Cash
Actors: Customer (Primary), Bank System (Secondary)
Preconditions: Customer has valid ATM card, account has sufficient balance
Basic Flow: 1) Insert card, 2) Enter PIN, 3) Select withdrawal, 4) Enter amount, 5) Confirm, 6) Dispense cash, 7) Return card
Alternative Flows: Invalid PIN, Insufficient balance, ATM out of cash, Network error, Cancel transaction
Postconditions: Cash dispensed, Account debited, Transaction logged
Business Rules: Max withdrawal limit, PIN attempts limited

questions.question: Explain the guidelines for writing effective use cases. What are the best practices to follow?
keyPoints: Keep use case name as verb-noun (e.g., Withdraw Money)
Focus on user goals, not system features
Write at appropriate level of detail (3-9 steps in basic flow)
Use consistent format and terminology
Include only relevant information, avoid UI details
Clearly distinguish between basic flow and alternative flows
Ensure preconditions and postconditions are testable
Use active voice and present tense
Name use cases to reflect achievement of actor's goal
Keep scope appropriate - not too granular or too broad

questions.question: Compare Use Cases with User Stories. How are they different? When would you use each?
keyPoints: Use Cases: Detailed structured description with multiple flows, formal notation, includes pre/post conditions, detailed scenarios
User Stories: Brief informal statement (As a... I want... So that...), focuses on value, limited detail
Use Cases: Good for complex systems, detailed requirements, contract between stakeholders
User Stories: Agile projects, quick prioritization, iterative development
Use Cases better for: regulated systems, complex business rules, detailed testing
User Stories better for: quick prototyping, agile teams, incremental delivery
Both can be used together in hybrid approaches
